edges.description: CSTC hyperactivity is modeled upstream of impaired behavioral inhibition and pathological habit formation.
edges.target: Impaired Inhibitory Control and Habit Formation

edges.description: Dopaminergic striatal signaling is represented upstream of circuit-level dysfunction.
Serotonergic dysregulation is represented upstream of circuit-level dysfunction.
Glutamatergic imbalance is represented upstream of circuit-level dysfunction.

edges.source: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy with Exposure and Response Prevention
edges.target: Impaired Inhibitory Control and Habit Formation

nodes.description: Adjunctive low-dose antipsychotic agents (e.g., risperidone, aripiprazole) are added to serotonin reuptake inhibitors in partial responders, and are the medication augmentation approach with the strongest empirical support in OCD.

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: Beyond SRI monotherapy, antipsychotic augmentation is the only medication approach for OCD with substantial empirical support.

nodes.description: Clomipramine, a serotonin-predominant tricyclic, was the first medication proven effective in OCD and remains a potent serotonin reuptake inhibitor option, typically reserved for SSRI non-responders due to its side-effect profile.

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: The only established first-line treatments for OCD are exposure and response prevention, and serotonin reuptake inhibitor medications (SRIs).

nodes.description: CBT incorporating exposure and response prevention (ERP) is a first-line psychotherapeutic treatment for OCD.
nodes.id: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy with Exposure and Response Prevention

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: The only established first-line treatments for OCD are exposure and response prevention, and serotonin reuptake inhibitor medications (SRIs).

nodes.description: OCD is associated with hyperactivity within cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) loops, particularly involving the orbitofrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, caudate nucleus, and thalamus. Imbalance between the direct ("go") and indirect ("stop") basal ganglia pathways is thought to impair the ability to suppress intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors.

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: Functional imaging studies in OCD show consistent evidence for increased activity in brain regions that form a cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) loop.

evidence.reference_title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder: Etiology, neuropathology, and cognitive dysfunction
evidence.snippet: We show how dysfunction in cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits may underpin symptoms

evidence.reference_title: Cortico-striatal synaptic defects and OCD-like behaviours in Sapap3-mutant mice
evidence.snippet: These findings demonstrate a critical role for SAPAP3 at cortico-striatal synapses and emphasize the importance of cortico-striatal circuitry in OCD-like behaviours.

nodes.description: Deep brain stimulation targeting nodes of the CSTC circuit is used for severe, treatment-refractory OCD, providing further support for the CSTC model.

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: Neuromodulation treatments with either noninvasive devices (e.g., transcranial magnetic stimulation) or invasive procedures (e.g., deep brain stimulation) provide further support for the CSTC model of OCD.

nodes.description: Dopaminergic signaling, particularly within the striatum, is implicated in OCD pathophysiology and in modulating compulsive behaviors. GWAS cell-type enrichment associates D1- and D2-receptor-containing striatal medium spiny neurons with OCD genetic risk.

evidence.reference_title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder: Etiology, neuropathology, and cognitive dysfunction
evidence.snippet: shed light on the putative neurochemistry within these loops such as the role of serotonin, dopamine, and glutamate systems

evidence.reference_title: Genome-wide analyses identify 30 loci associated with obsessive-compulsive disorder
evidence.snippet: OCD genetic risk was associated with excitatory neurons in the hippocampus and the cortex, along with D1 and D2 type dopamine receptor-containing medium spiny neurons.

nodes.description: Elevated glutamatergic neurotransmission within CSTC circuits, including altered glutamate transporter (SLC1A1/EAAT3) function, contributes to excitatory imbalance in OCD.

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: Several lines of preclinical and clinical evidence suggest dysfunction of the glutamatergic system in OCD, prompting testing of several promising glutamate modulating agents.

evidence.reference_title: OCD candidate gene SLC1A1/EAAT3 impacts basal ganglia-mediated activity and stereotypic behavior
evidence.snippet: these findings indicate that the most consistently associated OCD candidate gene impacts basal ganglia-dependent repetitive behaviors

nodes.description: The convergence of circuit and neurotransmitter abnormalities manifests as impaired top-down inhibitory control and a shift toward inflexible, habit-driven behavior, producing the obsessions and compulsions that characterize OCD. Cognitive deficits in response inhibition, cognitive flexibility, and goal-directed behavior are linked to aberrant CSTC activity.
nodes.id: Impaired Inhibitory Control and Habit Formation

evidence.reference_title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder: Etiology, neuropathology, and cognitive dysfunction
evidence.snippet: OCD is characterized by cognitive dysfunction including problems in cognitive flexibility, visuospatial memory, response inhibition, and goal-directed behavior, linked to aberrant activity within CSTC circuits

nodes.description: SSRIs (e.g., fluoxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine, paroxetine) are first-line pharmacotherapy for OCD, typically requiring higher doses and longer duration than for depression.

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: The only established first-line treatments for OCD are exposure and response prevention, and serotonin reuptake inhibitor medications (SRIs).

nodes.description: Dysfunction of serotonergic signaling is implicated in OCD, supported by the selective efficacy of serotonin reuptake inhibitors. Altered serotonin receptor and transporter function modulates CSTC circuit activity.

evidence.reference_title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder: Etiology, neuropathology, and cognitive dysfunction
evidence.snippet: shed light on the putative neurochemistry within these loops such as the role of serotonin, dopamine, and glutamate systems

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: The preferential efficacy of SRIs in OCD has neither led to discovery of serotonergic abnormalities in OCD nor to development of new serotonergic medications for OCD.

nodes.description: Repetitive/deep transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) is a noninvasive neuromodulation therapy, FDA-cleared for OCD, that targets medial prefrontal/anterior cingulate nodes of the CSTC circuit.

evidence.reference_title: Harmonizing the Neurobiology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
evidence.snippet: Neuromodulation treatments with either noninvasive devices (e.g., transcranial magnetic stimulation) or invasive procedures (e.g., deep brain stimulation) provide further support for the CSTC model of OCD.
